title: Detect cycle in an undirected graph (BFS with parent).
description: Detect cycle in an undirected graph (BFS with parent).

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency: {0:[1,3], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,0]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F]. Step 3: Start at vertex 0 (not visited). DFS(0, -1): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: 1 (not visited) → DFS(1,0): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 (visited but parent=0, ignore), 2 (not visited) → DFS(2,1): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore), 3 (not visited) → DFS(3,2): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 2 (visited, parent=2, ignore), 0 (visited AND parent=2 ≠ 0) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return true.

Step 1: adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F]. Step 3: Start at 0: DFS(0,-1): mark T, neighbors: 1 (not visited) → DFS(1,0): mark T, neighbors: 0 (visited, parent=0, ignore), 2 (not visited) → DFS(2,1): mark T, neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore). Return false. No cycle found from any vertex.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from each vertex to check for cycles by tracking visited nodes and parent nodes, restarting for disconnected components. It is straightforward but less efficient for large graphs due to repeated traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in undirected graphs using DFS from each vertex with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires detecting whether an undirected graph contains any cycle. The brute force DFS approach works by performing DFS from every vertex, treating each as a potential starting point for cycle detection. For each DFS traversal, we maintain a visited set and track parent nodes to distinguish between back edges (which indicate cycles) and normal parent-child relationships. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if during DFS we encounter an already visited node that is NOT the immediate parent of the current node. This indicates we've found an alternative path back to a previously visited node, forming a cycle. We must restart DFS for disconnected components since cycles might exist in isolated parts of the graph. The algorithm: 1) Create adjacency list from edges. 2) Initialize visited array. 3) For each vertex, if not visited, perform DFS with parent tracking. 4) In DFS, mark current node visited. 5) For each neighbor: if not visited, recursively DFS with current as parent; if visited AND neighbor ≠ parent, cycle detected. 6) Return true if any DFS finds cycle. Time: O(V*(V+E)) because we might traverse entire graph from each vertex in worst case (complete graph). Space: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This integrates the DFS cycle check subroutine across all vertices. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs, forgetting parent tracking, confusing undirected vs directed graph cycles. Real pattern: adjacency list, visited array, DFS recursion with parent parameter.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency: {0:[1,3], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,0]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F]. Step 3: Start at vertex 0 (not visited). DFS(0, -1): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: 1 (not visited) → DFS(1,0): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 (visited but parent=0, ignore), 2 (not visited) → DFS(2,1): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore), 3 (not visited) → DFS(3,2): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 2 (visited, parent=2, ignore), 0 (visited AND parent=2 ≠ 0) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return true.

Step 1: adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F]. Step 3: Start at 0: DFS(0,-1): mark T, neighbors: 1 (not visited) → DFS(1,0): mark T, neighbors: 0 (visited, parent=0, ignore), 2 (not visited) → DFS(2,1): mark T, neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore). Return false. No cycle found from any vertex.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Cycle Check from a Single Source
ladders.desc: DFS function that detects cycles starting from a given vertex with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs DFS from a specific vertex to check for cycles in its connected component. The core idea is to traverse the graph depth-first while keeping track of visited nodes and the parent of each node. In undirected graphs, each edge is bidirectional, so when we traverse from node A to B, B's parent is A. A cycle exists if we encounter a neighbor that is already visited AND that neighbor is not the immediate parent. This indicates there's an alternative path back to that neighbor, forming a cycle. The algorithm: function dfs(node, parent): 1) Mark node as visited. 2) For each neighbor in adjacency[node]: if neighbor not visited, recursively call dfs(neighbor, node). If that returns true, propagate true. If neighbor visited AND neighbor ≠ parent, return true (cycle found). 3) Return false if no cycle detected. Time: O(V+E) for the connected component. Space: O(V) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called for each unvisited vertex in the main problem. Common mistakes: Not passing parent correctly, missing base case for parent=-1 (start node), not handling recursion return values properly. Real pattern: recursive DFS with visited set and parent parameter.

examples.input: Start node=0, parent=-1, adjacency={0:[1,2],1:[0,2],2:[0,1]}, visited initially all false
examples.trace: dfs(0,-1): mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (not visited) → dfs(1,0): mark 1 visited, neighbors: 0 (visited but parent=0, ignore), 2 (not visited) → dfs(2,1): mark 2 visited, neighbors: 0 (visited AND parent=1 ≠ 0) → CYCLE, return true. Propagate true up.

examples.input: Start node=0, parent=-1, adjacency={0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1]}, visited all false
examples.trace: dfs(0,-1): mark 0 visited. Neighbor: 1 (not visited) → dfs(1,0): mark 1 visited, neighbors: 0 (visited, parent=0, ignore), 2 (not visited) → dfs(2,1): mark 2 visited, neighbor: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore). Return false. No cycle.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Back Edges in Undirected DFS
ladders.desc: The condition that identifies cycles: visited neighbor that is not the parent.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight for cycle detection in undirected graphs using DFS. During DFS traversal, we maintain a visited set and track the parent of each node (the node we came from). When exploring neighbors of the current node, if we find a neighbor that is already visited, we must check: is this neighbor the parent? If yes, this is just the edge we came from (A→B, then B→A), which is normal and doesn't indicate a cycle. If the visited neighbor is NOT the parent, this means we've found an alternative path back to a node already in the current DFS path, which forms a cycle. This works because in undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional, so the only legitimate visited neighbor should be the immediate parent. Any other visited neighbor indicates a back edge. The algorithm step: for each neighbor: if not visited: recurse; else if neighbor ≠ parent: return true. Time: O(1) per edge check. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This condition is the key check within the DFS function. Common mistakes: Forgetting to exclude parent, confusing with directed graphs where cycles are detected differently. Real pattern: if visited[neighbor] and neighbor != parent: return True.

examples.trace: Check neighbors: 1 is visited but parent=1 (ignore), 3 not visited (recurse later), 0 is visited AND parent=1 ≠ 0 → condition true, cycle detected.
Check neighbors: 0 visited but parent=0 (ignore), 2 not visited (recurse). No neighbor satisfies visited AND ≠ parent, so false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with DFS and Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on a graph while marking nodes as visited to avoid reprocessing.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a graph using Depth-First Search while maintaining a visited set to ensure each node is processed only once. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For graphs, we typically use recursion or a stack. The visited set prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs and ensures termination. The algorithm: 1) Start at a node, mark visited. 2) For each neighbor not visited, recursively perform DFS. This builds a DFS tree covering the connected component. Time: O(V+E) for the component. Space: O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. Connection to L2: This traversal provides the framework where we add parent tracking and cycle detection. Common mistakes: Not marking node as visited before recursing (causing infinite recursion), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs. Real pattern: visited array, adjacency list, recursive function.

examples.input: Start node=0, adjacency={0:[1,2],1:[0,3],2:[0],3:[1]}, visited initially all false
examples.trace: dfs(0): mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (not visited) → dfs(1): mark 1 visited, neighbors: 0 (visited, skip), 3 (not visited) → dfs(3): mark 3 visited, neighbor: 1 (visited, skip). Back to 0: neighbor 2 (not visited) → dfs(2): mark 2 visited, neighbor: 0 (visited, skip). Done.

examples.input: Start node=0, adjacency={0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}, visited all false
examples.trace: dfs(0): mark 0 visited, neighbor: 1 → dfs(1): mark 1 visited, neighbors: 0 (skip), 2 → dfs(2): mark 2 visited, neighbors: 1 (skip), 3 → dfs(3): mark 3 visited, neighbor: 2 (skip). Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Graph Neighbors
ladders.desc: Access and process all adjacent nodes of a given vertex in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves retrieving and iterating over all neighbors of a vertex in a graph. Graphs are typically represented using adjacency lists (array of lists) or adjacency matrices. For cycle detection, we use adjacency lists for efficiency. Given a vertex v, we access its list of neighbors from the adjacency data structure, then loop through each neighbor to perform operations (e.g., check if visited, recurse). This is basic but crucial for graph algorithms. The intuition: graphs model connections, and processing neighbors is how we traverse these connections. Algorithm: for neighbor in adjacency[v]: do something. Time: O(degree(v)) per vertex. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within DFS to explore connected nodes. Common mistakes: Using wrong index, not handling empty neighbor lists, assuming symmetric edges in directed graphs. Real pattern: adjacency list as list of lists, for loop over adjacency[node].

examples.trace: adjacency[1] = [0,2,3]. Iterate: first neighbor 0, then 2, then 3.
adjacency[0] = [1]. Iterate: neighbor 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation with Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a graph as a list where each index stores neighbors of that vertex.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: representing an undirected graph using an adjacency list. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections). For V vertices, we create a list of size V, where each element is a list of integers representing the neighbors of that vertex. For example, if edge (0,1) exists, we add 1 to adjacency[0] and 0 to adjacency[1] because the graph is undirected. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to neighbors. Intuition: it's like a friendship list where each person has a list of friends. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty list of size V. 2) For each edge (u,v): append v to adjacency[u], append u to adjacency[v]. Time: O(E) to build. Space: O(V+E). Connection to L4: This data structure enables neighbor iteration. Common mistakes: Not initializing lists properly, forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, index out of bounds. Real pattern: adjacency = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adjacency[u].append(v); adjacency[v].append(u).

approaches.name: BFS with Parent O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use BFS to traverse the graph, keeping track of parent nodes to avoid back edges to immediate parents. If a visited neighbor is not the parent, a cycle is detected. It is efficient and works well for undirected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using BFS with Parent
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in undirected graphs using BFS with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires detecting whether an undirected graph contains any cycles. The BFS with parent approach works by performing breadth-first search while maintaining parent information for each visited node. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, during BFS traversal, if we encounter a visited neighbor that is not the immediate parent of the current node, we've found a cycle. This works because in BFS, we explore nodes level by level, and any edge connecting nodes at the same level or to an already visited node (except the parent) indicates a back edge forming a cycle.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Create an adjacency list representation of the graph
2. Initialize visited array with False for all vertices
3. For each unvisited vertex:
   a. Create a queue and enqueue the current vertex with parent = -1
   b. Mark current vertex as visited
   c. While queue is not empty:
      i. Dequeue current node and its parent
      ii. For each neighbor of current node:
          - If neighbor is not visited: mark visited, enqueue (neighbor, current)
          - Else if neighbor is visited AND neighbor ≠ parent: return True (cycle found)
4. If no cycles found after processing all vertices, return False

Time complexity: O(V + E) where V is vertices, E is edges. We visit each vertex once and each edge once.
Space complexity: O(V) for visited array, queue, and adjacency list.

Connection to L1: This full solution uses the BFS traversal with parent tracking as its core subroutine.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need to check all components)
2. Not properly handling the parent relationship (comparing with immediate parent only)
3. Using directed graph logic on undirected graphs
4. Not resetting parent for each BFS call on different components

Real code pattern:
def has_cycle_bfs(graph, n):
    visited = [False] * n
    for i in range(n):
        if not visited[i]:
            queue = deque()
            queue.append((i, -1))
            visited[i] = True
            while queue:
                node, parent = queue.popleft()
                for neighbor in graph[node]:
                    if not visited[neighbor]:
                        visited[neighbor] = True
                        queue.append((neighbor, node))
                    elif neighbor != parent:
                        return True
    return False

examples.trace:
Graph: 0-1-2-3-4 with edge 4-1 creating cycle 1-2-3-4-1
BFS starting at 0:
Queue: [(0,-1)], visited[0]=True
Process 0: neighbors=[1], neighbor 1 not visited → queue[(1,0)], visited[1]=True
Process 1: neighbors=[0,2,4], parent=0
  neighbor 0: visited but 0=parent ✓
  neighbor 2: not visited → queue[(2,1)], visited[2]=True
  neighbor 4: not visited → queue[(4,1)], visited[4]=True
Process 2: neighbors=[1,3], parent=1
  neighbor 1: visited but 1=parent ✓
  neighbor 3: not visited → queue[(3,2)], visited[3]=True
Process 4: neighbors=[3,1], parent=1
  neighbor 3: visited and 3≠1 → CYCLE DETECTED
Return True

Graph: 0-1-2-3 (linear chain)
BFS starting at 0:
Queue: [(0,-1)], visited[0]=True
Process 0: neighbors=[1], neighbor 1 not visited → queue[(1,0)], visited[1]=True
Process 1: neighbors=[0,2], parent=0
  neighbor 0: visited and 0=parent ✓
  neighbor 2: not visited → queue[(2,1)], visited[2]=True
Process 2: neighbors=[1,3], parent=1
  neighbor 1: visited and 1=parent ✓
  neighbor 3: not visited → queue[(3,2)], visited[3]=True
Process 3: neighbors=[2], parent=2
  neighbor 2: visited and 2=parent ✓
Queue empty, all vertices visited, no cycle found
Return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement BFS that tracks parent nodes to distinguish between parent-child edges and cycle-forming edges.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine performs BFS traversal while maintaining parent information for each node. The intuition is that during graph traversal, we need to know which node discovered the current node to avoid mistaking the immediate parent edge for a cycle. In undirected graphs, each edge is bidirectional, so when we traverse from node A to node B, we mark B's parent as A. Later, when processing B's neighbors, we'll see edge B-A, but since A is B's parent, we know this is not a cycle—it's just the edge we came from.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: starting vertex, adjacency list, visited array
2. Initialize queue with (start_vertex, -1) where -1 indicates no parent
3. Mark start_vertex as visited
4. While queue not empty:
   a. Dequeue (current, parent)
   b. For each neighbor in adjacency[current]:
      i. If neighbor not visited: mark visited, enqueue (neighbor, current)
      ii. Else if neighbor visited AND neighbor ≠ parent: return True (cycle)
5. Return False if queue empties without finding cycle

Time complexity: O(V + E) for the component being traversed.
Space complexity: O(V) for queue and visited tracking.

Connection to L0: This is the core traversal logic used in the full solution, called for each connected component.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to pass parent information in the queue
2. Not handling the initial parent (-1) correctly
3. Comparing with wrong parent when checking visited neighbors
4. Not marking nodes as visited before enqueuing (causing duplicates)

Real code pattern:
def bfs_with_parent(start, graph, visited):
    queue = deque()
    queue.append((start, -1))
    visited[start] = True
    while queue:
        node, parent = queue.popleft()
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            if not visited[neighbor]:
                visited[neighbor] = True
                queue.append((neighbor, node))
            elif neighbor != parent:
                return True
    return False

examples.trace:
Queue: [(0,-1)], visited[0]=True
Process 0: neighbors=[1,2], parent=-1
  neighbor 1: not visited → queue[(1,0)], visited[1]=True
  neighbor 2: not visited → queue[(2,0)], visited[2]=True
Process 1: neighbors=[0,2], parent=0
  neighbor 0: visited but 0=parent ✓
  neighbor 2: visited and 2≠0 → CYCLE DETECTED
Return True

Queue: [(0,-1)], visited[0]=True
Process 0: neighbors=[1], parent=-1
  neighbor 1: not visited → queue[(1,0)], visited[1]=True
Process 1: neighbors=[0,2], parent=0
  neighbor 0: visited and 0=parent ✓
  neighbor 2: not visited → queue[(2,1)], visited[2]=True
Process 2: neighbors=[1], parent=1
  neighbor 1: visited and 1=parent ✓
Queue empty, no cycle found
Return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Back Edges in Undirected BFS
ladders.desc: Identify when a visited neighbor indicates a cycle by checking if it's not the immediate parent.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic focuses on the key insight: in BFS traversal of an undirected graph, a cycle exists if and only if we encounter a visited neighbor that is not the immediate parent of the current node. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level. When we process a node, we look at its neighbors. If a neighbor is already visited, there are two possibilities: 1) It's our parent (the node we came from), which is normal traversal, or 2) It's not our parent, meaning we've found an alternative path to this node, creating a cycle.

Step-by-step logic:
1. During BFS, for current node with parent P
2. For each neighbor N of current node:
   a. If N is not visited: continue normal BFS
   b. If N is visited: check if N == P
      i. If N == P: this is the edge we came from (not a cycle)
      ii. If N ≠ P: we've found a back edge forming a cycle

Time complexity: O(1) per edge check.
Space complexity: O(1) for the comparison.

Connection to L1: This is the critical condition check within the BFS traversal subroutine.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to check parent condition entirely
2. Checking if neighbor is parent before checking if visited
3. Not handling the root node's parent (-1) correctly
4. Confusing directed graph logic (where any visited neighbor indicates cycle)

Real code pattern:
for neighbor in graph[node]:
    if not visited[neighbor]:
        # Normal BFS expansion
        visited[neighbor] = True
        queue.append((neighbor, node))
    elif neighbor != parent:
        # CYCLE DETECTED
        return True

examples.trace:
Processing node 2 with parent 1
Neighbor 1: visited and 1==parent → normal edge
Neighbor 3: not visited → would expand normally
Neighbor 4: visited and 4≠1 → CYCLE DETECTED
Return True

Processing node 3 with parent 2
Neighbor 2: visited and 2==parent → normal edge
Neighbor 4: visited but need to check: 4≠2 → would detect cycle if this check is made
But wait: visited[4] is True, and 4≠2, so this WOULD detect a cycle
Let me correct: Actually this should return True if visited[4]=True
Let's use different example:
Current node=1, parent=0, neighbors=[0,2], visited=[True,True,False]
Neighbor 0: visited and 0==parent → normal
Neighbor 2: not visited → normal expansion
No cycle detected → Return False

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and BFS Queue Management
ladders.desc: Create adjacency list from edges and implement BFS queue operations with tuple elements.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves two essential components: 1) Converting edge lists into adjacency list representation suitable for BFS, and 2) Managing a queue that stores both node and parent information. The intuition is that we need an efficient way to traverse graph neighbors (adjacency list) and a data structure that remembers both the current node and where it came from (queue with tuples).

Step-by-step:
1. Adjacency list creation:
   a. Initialize empty list of size n
   b. For each edge [u,v]:
      i. Add v to adj[u]
      ii. Add u to adj[v] (undirected graph)
2. BFS queue management:
   a. Use deque for efficient O(1) operations
   b. Store tuples (node, parent)
   c. Enqueue: queue.append((node, parent))
   d. Dequeue: node, parent = queue.popleft()

Time complexity: O(E) for adjacency list creation, O(1) per queue operation.
Space complexity: O(V + E) for adjacency list, O(V) for queue.

Connection to L2: This provides the data structures needed to implement the cycle detection logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Creating directed adjacency list for undirected graph
2. Not initializing adjacency list properly for all vertices
3. Using list instead of deque for queue (inefficient pops)
4. Forgetting to store parent in queue tuples

Real code pattern:
# Create adjacency list
adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
for u, v in edges:
    adj[u].append(v)
    adj[v].append(u)

# BFS queue management
from collections import deque
queue = deque()
queue.append((start_node, -1))  # -1 for no parent
while queue:
    current, parent = queue.popleft()
    # Process current node

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Visited Status and Parent Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement the fundamental operations of marking nodes visited and comparing nodes with parents.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves two simple but crucial tasks: 1) Maintaining and checking a visited array to avoid reprocessing nodes, and 2) Comparing node identifiers to determine parent relationships. The intuition is that graph traversal requires remembering which nodes we've already seen (visited array) and understanding relationships between nodes (parent comparison). These are the atomic operations that make cycle detection possible.

Step-by-step:
1. Visited array operations:
   a. Initialize: visited = [False] * n
   b. Mark visited: visited[node] = True
   c. Check visited: if not visited[neighbor]:
2. Parent comparison:
   a. Store parent for each node during BFS
   b. Compare: if neighbor != parent:

Time complexity: O(1) for each operation.
Space complexity: O(V) for visited array.

Connection to L3: These operations are used within the BFS queue management and adjacency list traversal.

Common mistakes:
1. Using wrong index for visited array
2. Forgetting to mark nodes as visited before enqueuing
3. Comparing with wrong variable (parent vs current node)
4. Not handling -1 parent for root node correctly

Real code pattern:
# Initialize visited array
visited = [False] * n

# Mark node as visited
visited[node] = True

# Check if neighbor is visited
if not visited[neighbor]:
    # Process unvisited node

# Compare with parent
if neighbor != parent:
    # Potential cycle

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Nodes, Edges, and Boolean Arrays
ladders.desc: Understand basic graph concepts: vertices, edges, and using arrays to track states.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation covers the simplest prerequisites: 1) Understanding graphs as collections of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections), and 2) Using boolean arrays to track states. The intuition is that all graph algorithms build on these basic concepts. Vertices are the fundamental units, edges define relationships between them, and boolean arrays let us remember information about each vertex (like whether we've visited it).

Key concepts:
1. Vertices/Nodes: Represented as integers 0 to n-1
2. Edges: Pairs of vertices [u,v] representing connections
3. Boolean arrays: Lists of True/False values, one per vertex

Operations:
1. Access array element: visited[vertex_index]
2. Modify array element: visited[vertex_index] = True
3. Compare values: vertex_a != vertex_b

Time complexity: O(1) for array access/modification.
Space complexity: O(n) for array storage.

Connection to L4: These fundamental concepts enable the visited tracking and parent comparison operations.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing
2. Not allocating enough space in arrays
3. Comparing vertex values incorrectly
4. Misunderstanding that edges are bidirectional in undirected graphs

Real code pattern:
# n vertices
n = 5

# Create boolean array
visited = [False] * n  # [False, False, False, False, False]

# Access and modify
visited[0] = True  # Mark vertex 0 as visited

# Check status
if visited[2]:  # Check if vertex 2 is visited
    print("Vertex 2 is visited")

# Compare vertices
if vertex_a != vertex_b:
    print("Different vertices")

examples.input: n=4, mark vertices 0 and 2 as visited, check vertex 3
examples.trace:
Create boolean array for 4 vertices: visited = [False, False, False, False]
Mark vertex 0: visited[0] = True → [True, False, False, False]
Mark vertex 2: visited[2] = True → [True, False, True, False]
Check vertex 3: visited[3] returns False (not visited)
Check vertex 0: visited[0] returns True (visited)

We have 3 vertices: 0, 1, 2
Edges connect: 0-1 and 1-2
Create boolean array: [False, False, False]
Compare vertices 1 and 2: 1 != 2 returns True (different vertices)
If we had edge [1,1], that would be self-loop (1=1 returns False)

testCases.input: n=3, mark vertices 0 and 2, check vertex 2
n=4, mark vertices 0,3, check vertex 2, compare 3 and 0

approaches.name: DFS with Parent O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS recursively or iteratively, marking visited nodes and checking for cycles by ensuring neighbors are not visited except the parent. It is optimal and commonly used for cycle detection in undirected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using DFS with Parent
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in undirected graphs using DFS with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete solution for detecting cycles in undirected graphs using DFS with parent tracking. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if during DFS traversal, we encounter a visited node that is not the immediate parent of the current node. This works because in undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional, so when we traverse from node A to node B, we should only allow revisiting A from B (the parent), but if we encounter any other visited node from B, it means there's an alternative path back to that node, forming a cycle.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Create a visited array/map to track visited nodes
2. For each unvisited node in the graph:
   a. Start DFS from that node with parent = -1 (no parent)
   b. Mark current node as visited
   c. For each neighbor of current node:
      - If neighbor is not visited: recursively call DFS with current node as parent
      - If neighbor is visited AND neighbor ≠ parent: cycle detected
3. Return true if any cycle is found, false otherwise

Time complexity: O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as we visit each node and edge once.
Space complexity: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack.

Connection to L1: This level combines the DFS traversal from L1 with the parent tracking logic to form the complete solution.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need to check all components)
2. Not properly handling the parent parameter (especially for the starting node)
3. Confusing directed vs undirected graph cycle detection logic

Real code pattern:
def has_cycle(graph):
    visited = [False] * len(graph)
    
    def dfs(node, parent):
        visited[node] = True
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            if not visited[neighbor]:
                if dfs(neighbor, node):
                    return True
            elif neighbor != parent:
                return True
        return False
    
    for node in range(len(graph)):
        if not visited[node]:
            if dfs(node, -1):
                return True
    return False

examples.trace: Start DFS from node 0 (parent = -1). Mark 0 visited. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1, 0). Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited but 0 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2, 1). Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 1: visited but 1 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 3: not visited, call dfs(3, 2). Mark 3 visited. Check neighbor 2: visited but 2 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 0: visited AND 0 ≠ parent (parent is 2), cycle detected! Return True up the call stack.

Start DFS from node 0 (parent = -1). Mark 0 visited. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1, 0). Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited but 0 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2, 1). Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 1: visited but 1 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 3: not visited, call dfs(3, 2). Mark 3 visited. Check neighbor 2: visited but 2 = parent, continue. No more neighbors. Return False up the call stack. Check all nodes visited, return False.

testCases.input: Graph: Single node 0 with no edges

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: The recursive DFS function that tracks parent nodes during traversal.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core recursive DFS function that performs the actual traversal while tracking parent relationships. The intuition is that we need to explore the graph depth-first while remembering where we came from (the parent) to distinguish between legitimate back-edges (to parent) and cycle-forming back-edges (to non-parent visited nodes).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Mark current node as visited
2. For each neighbor of current node:
   a. If neighbor is not visited: recursively call DFS with current node as parent
   b. If neighbor is visited AND neighbor ≠ parent: return true (cycle found)
3. Return false if no cycle found in this subtree

Time complexity: O(V + E) for the complete traversal of the connected component.
Space complexity: O(V) for recursion stack depth.

Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly from the main function in L0 to explore all connected components.

Connection to L2: This implements the cycle detection logic from L2 within a recursive DFS framework.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to pass the parent parameter correctly
2. Not returning the result of recursive calls
3. Incorrect base case handling

Real code pattern:
def dfs(node, parent, visited, graph):
    visited[node] = True
    for neighbor in graph[node]:
        if not visited[neighbor]:
            if dfs(neighbor, node, visited, graph):
                return True
        elif neighbor != parent:
            return True
    return False

examples.trace: dfs(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1, 0). dfs(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited but 0 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2, 1). dfs(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 1: visited but 1 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 0: visited AND 0 ≠ parent (parent is 1), return True. True propagates back up.

dfs(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1, 0). dfs(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited but 0 = parent, continue. Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2, 1). dfs(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 1: visited but 1 = parent, continue. No more neighbors, return False. False propagates back up.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection via Parent Check
ladders.desc: The fundamental insight: a cycle exists if we encounter a visited node that is not the parent.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central insight of the algorithm. In an undirected graph, when performing DFS, if we encounter a neighbor that has already been visited AND that neighbor is not the node we just came from (the parent), then we've found a cycle. The intuition is that in a tree (which is a cycle-free undirected graph), the only visited node we should encounter when exploring from a node is its parent (via the edge we just traversed). If we find any other visited node, it means there's an alternative path to reach that node, creating a cycle.

Why this works:
1. In DFS traversal of an undirected graph, we're essentially building a spanning tree
2. Any edge that connects a node to a non-parent visited node creates a cycle
3. This is because there are now two different paths between the nodes

Step-by-step logic:
For current node u with parent p:
For each neighbor v of u:
   If v is not visited: continue DFS
   Else if v is visited and v ≠ p: CYCLE DETECTED

Time complexity: O(1) per edge check.
Space complexity: O(1) for the comparison.

Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the DFS function's neighbor checking loop.

Connection to L3: This builds upon the concept of graph traversal from L3 by adding the cycle detection condition.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting the 'neighbor ≠ parent' condition
2. Applying this logic to directed graphs (which requires different cycle detection)
3. Not understanding why parent is excluded from cycle detection

Real code pattern:
for neighbor in graph[node]:
    if not visited[neighbor]:
        # continue traversal
    elif neighbor != parent:
        return True  # cycle detected

examples.trace: Check neighbor 1: visited but 1 = parent (okay). Check neighbor 3: not visited (continue). Check neighbor 0: visited AND 0 ≠ parent (parent is 1). Cycle detected! Return True.
Check neighbor 1: visited but 1 = parent (okay). Check neighbor 3: not visited (continue). No cycle detected. Return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Basic DFS traversal of a graph while marking visited nodes to avoid reprocessing.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental graph traversal technique that forms the backbone of our algorithm. The intuition is that we need to systematically explore all nodes in the graph without visiting any node more than once. We use a visited array to track which nodes we've already processed. In DFS, we explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.

Why visited tracking is essential:
1. Prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs
2. Ensures each node is processed exactly once
3. Enables efficient O(V+E) traversal

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize visited array with False for all nodes
2. Define recursive DFS function:
   a. Mark current node as visited
   b. Process current node (in our case, check neighbors)
   c. For each unvisited neighbor: recursively call DFS
3. For disconnected graphs: call DFS on each unvisited node

Time complexity: O(V + E) - each node and edge visited once.
Space complexity: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack.

Connection to L2: This traversal provides the framework where we'll add the parent checking logic for cycle detection.

Connection to L4: This builds upon the adjacency list access from L4 to perform systematic traversal.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to mark nodes as visited before recursive calls
2. Not handling disconnected graphs
3. Using the wrong data structure for visited tracking

Real code pattern:
visited = [False] * n

def dfs(node):
    visited[node] = True
    for neighbor in graph[node]:
        if not visited[neighbor]:
            dfs(neighbor)

for i in range(n):
    if not visited[i]:
        dfs(i)

examples.trace: Start dfs(0): Mark 0 visited. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1). dfs(1): Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited (skip). Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). dfs(2): Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 1: visited (skip). Return. Return. Return.

Start dfs(0): Mark 0 visited. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1). dfs(1): Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited (skip). Check neighbor 3: not visited, call dfs(3). dfs(3): Mark 3 visited. Return. Back to dfs(0): Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). dfs(2): Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited (skip). Return.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Graph Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of retrieving neighbors from a graph representation.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most basic operation in graph algorithms: accessing the adjacency list of a node. The intuition is that graphs are typically represented as adjacency lists (array of lists) where graph[i] contains all neighbors of node i. This representation is efficient for traversal algorithms because it allows O(1) access to a node's neighbors and O(degree(i)) time to iterate through them.

Why adjacency lists are preferred for DFS:
1. Space efficient: O(V + E) space
2. Fast neighbor iteration: O(degree(v)) per node
3. Easy to implement and understand

Step-by-step operation:
1. Graph is represented as: graph = [[neighbors_of_0], [neighbors_of_1], ...]
2. To get neighbors of node u: neighbors = graph[u]
3. To iterate through neighbors: for v in graph[u]:
4. To check if v is neighbor of u: v in graph[u] (though linear search)

Time complexity: O(1) to access the adjacency list, O(degree(u)) to iterate neighbors.
Space complexity: O(V + E) for storing the graph.

Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in the DFS traversal to explore neighboring nodes.

Connection to L5: This builds upon the array/list access concepts from L5.

Common mistakes:
1. Using wrong index (off-by-one errors)
2. Assuming symmetric edges in directed graphs
3. Not handling empty adjacency lists (isolated nodes)

Real code pattern:
# Graph representation
graph = [
    [1, 2],    # Node 0 neighbors
    [0, 2],    # Node 1 neighbors
    [0, 1]     # Node 2 neighbors
]

# Access neighbors of node 0
neighbors_of_0 = graph[0]  # Returns [1, 2]

# Iterate through neighbors
for neighbor in graph[0]:
    print(neighbor)  # Prints 1, then 2

examples.trace: graph[1] returns the list at index 1, which is [0, 2]. These are the neighbors of node 1. Node 1 is connected to nodes 0 and 2.
graph[3] returns the list at index 3, which is [1]. Node 3 is only connected to node 1. This is a star-like structure centered at node 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays/Lists and Basic Iteration
ladders.desc: Fundamental programming concepts: arrays/lists creation, access, and iteration.
ladders.explanation:
This is the absolute foundation: understanding arrays (lists in Python) and basic iteration. The intuition is that we need a way to store and access collections of data. Arrays provide indexed access to elements, while iteration allows us to process each element sequentially. These are the building blocks for representing graphs (as arrays of adjacency lists) and traversing them.

Key concepts:
1. Array/list creation: arr = [value1, value2, ...] or arr = [0] * n
2. Indexed access: arr[i] gets/sets element at position i (0-based)
3. Iteration: for element in arr: or for i in range(len(arr)):
4. Nested lists: lists containing other lists (like adjacency lists)

Why this matters for graph algorithms:
1. Graphs are represented as arrays of adjacency lists
2. We need to iterate through neighbors during traversal
3. We need to track visited nodes using arrays

Step-by-step basics:
1. Create a list: visited = [False, False, False]
2. Access element: visited[0] returns False
3. Modify element: visited[0] = True
4. Iterate: for i in range(3): print(visited[i])
5. Nested list: graph = [[1,2], [0], [0]]

Time complexity: O(1) for access, O(n) for iteration.
Space complexity: O(n) for storing n elements.

Connection to L4: These basic operations are used to create and access the adjacency list representation.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors in indices
2. Modifying list while iterating
3. Confusing list assignment with copying

Real code pattern:
# Create a list
my_list = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Access elements
first = my_list[0]  # 1
last = my_list[-1]  # 5

# Modify elements
my_list[0] = 10

# Iterate through list
for item in my_list:
    print(item)

# Iterate with index
for i in range(len(my_list)):
    print(f"Index {i}: {my_list[i]}")

# Create nested list (like adjacency list)
graph = [[1, 2], [0, 3], [0], [1]]

examples.input: Create list [0, 1, 2, 3], access index 2, modify index 0 to 5
examples.trace:
1. Create list: arr = [0, 1, 2, 3]
2. Access index 2: arr[2] returns 2
3. Modify index 0: arr[0] = 5
4. List becomes: [5, 1, 2, 3]

examples.input: List: [10, 20, 30, 40], iterate and print each element
examples.trace:
for item in [10, 20, 30, 40]:
    print(item)
Output: 10 (first iteration)
Output: 20 (second iteration)
Output: 30 (third iteration)
Output: 40 (fourth iteration)

approaches.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set) O(E log V)
approaches.summary: Use a disjoint set data structure to union vertices as edges are processed; if two vertices of an edge are already in the same set, a cycle is detected. It is efficient for dynamic graphs and avoids explicit traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in undirected graphs using Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: The Union-Find approach detects cycles in undirected graphs by maintaining connected components. Each vertex starts in its own set. As we process edges, we union the sets of the two vertices. If both vertices of an edge are already in the same set before unioning, we've found a cycle because there already exists a path between them. This works because in an undirected graph, any edge connecting two vertices in the same connected component creates a cycle. The algorithm processes all edges once, making it efficient for sparse graphs. Time complexity is O(E log V) due to union operations with path compression and union by rank. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. The key insight is that cycles form when we try to connect vertices that are already connected through other paths. This approach is particularly useful for dynamic graphs where edges are added incrementally. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize parent arrays, not implementing path compression properly, and misunderstanding that we check for same parent BEFORE unioning (not after). The algorithm works for both connected and disconnected graphs.

examples.trace:
Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]
Edge (0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1 (different) → union(0,1): parent=[0,0,2,3], rank=[1,0,0,0]
Edge (1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2 (different) → union(0,2): parent=[0,0,0,3], rank=[1,0,0,0]
Edge (2,3): find(2)=0, find(3)=3 (different) → union(0,3): parent=[0,0,0,0], rank=[1,0,0,0]
Edge (3,0): find(3)=0, find(0)=0 (SAME) → CYCLE DETECTED!

Initialize: parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0]
Edge (0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1 (different) → union(0,1): parent=[0,0,2], rank=[1,0,0]
Edge (1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2 (different) → union(0,2): parent=[0,0,0], rank=[1,0,0]
All edges processed, no cycle found.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find Operations with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implementation of find() and union() operations with optimization techniques.
ladders.explanation: The Union-Find data structure relies on two fundamental operations: find() to determine which set an element belongs to, and union() to merge two sets. Find operation uses path compression - as we traverse up to find the root, we flatten the tree by making each node point directly to the root. This optimization ensures near-constant time amortized complexity. Union operation uses union by rank/size - we attach the smaller tree under the larger tree to keep the tree balanced. The intuition is that by keeping trees flat and balanced, we minimize the time for future operations. Time complexity for each operation is O(α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant). Space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by providing the efficient set operations needed for cycle detection. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression recursively (which can cause stack overflow) or forgetting to update ranks during union. The key insight is that these optimizations make Union-Find extremely efficient for dynamic connectivity problems.

examples.input: Initial: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(1), union(1,3)
examples.trace:
union(0,1): root0=0, root1=1, rank0=0, rank1=0 → parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1
union(2,3): root2=2, root3=3, rank2=0, rank3=0 → parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1
find(1): parent[1]=0, parent[0]=0 → return 0 (with path compression: parent[1]=0)
union(1,3): find(1)=0, find(3)=2, rank0=1, rank2=1 → parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2

examples.input: Initial: parent=[0,0,1,2,3], rank=[2,1,1,1,0]. Operations: find(4), find(3), union(0,4)
examples.trace:
find(4): parent[4]=3, parent[3]=2, parent[2]=1, parent[1]=0, parent[0]=0 → return 0 (path compression sets parent[4]=0, parent[3]=0, parent[2]=0)
find(3): parent[3]=0 (from compression) → return 0
union(0,4): find(0)=0, find(4)=0 (same) → no union needed

testCases.input: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], operations: union(0,1), union(0,2), union(0,3), union(0,4)
parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5], operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), union(3,5)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection Using Set Connectivity
ladders.desc: The central insight: if two vertices of an edge are in the same set, a cycle exists.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of Union-Find cycle detection is beautifully simple: process each edge, check if its endpoints belong to the same connected component. If they do, you've found a cycle. If not, union their components. This works because in an undirected graph, any additional edge between vertices that are already connected creates exactly one new cycle. The intuition comes from graph theory: a tree (acyclic graph) with V vertices has exactly V-1 edges. Adding one more edge to a connected component creates exactly one cycle. Union-Find maintains these connected components dynamically. As we process edges in any order, we're essentially building the graph incrementally. When we encounter an edge whose endpoints are already connected via some path (same set), that edge completes a cycle. Time complexity is O(E log V) for processing E edges. Space is O(V). This logic feeds into the full implementation by providing the algorithmic framework. Common mistakes include processing edges in wrong order (order doesn't matter for undirected graphs) or checking for cycle after union (must check before). The key insight is that we're detecting when an edge is redundant for connectivity.

examples.trace:
Process (0,1): sets {0,1}, {2} (union)
Process (1,2): sets {0,1,2} (union)
Process (2,0): both in {0,1,2} → CYCLE

ladders.title: Building Block — Connected Components in Graphs
ladders.desc: Understanding how vertices group into connected sets in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: In undirected graphs, vertices are grouped into connected components - subsets where every pair of vertices is connected by a path. A graph can have one component (connected graph) or multiple components (disconnected graph). Understanding components is crucial because cycles can only exist within a component (an edge between components cannot create a cycle). The Union-Find algorithm naturally maintains these components: initially each vertex is its own component, and when we union two vertices, we merge their components. The key properties: 1) Within a component, there exists at least one path between any two vertices. 2) Different components have no connections between them. 3) Adding an edge within a component may create a cycle. 4) Adding an edge between components never creates a cycle (it just merges them). Time to identify all components is O(V+E) with BFS/DFS, or O(E log V) with Union-Find. Space is O(V). This concept feeds into cycle detection because we only need to check edges within components. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected connectivity, or not understanding that components partition the vertex set. The intuition is that components are equivalence classes under the 'is reachable from' relation.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Set Union and Find Operations
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of merging sets and checking membership.
ladders.explanation: Set union and find are fundamental operations in computer science. Union merges two sets into one, while find determines which set an element belongs to. In the context of Union-Find, we maintain a forest of trees where each tree represents a set, and the root is the representative element. Find(x) follows parent pointers to the root. Union(x,y) makes the root of one tree point to the root of the other. The naive implementation takes O(n) time per operation in worst case. The intuition is that we're maintaining equivalence classes: elements in the same set are equivalent (connected). These operations form the foundation for many algorithms including Kruskal's MST, connected components, and cycle detection. Time complexity for naive implementation is O(n) per operation, optimized to O(α(n)) with path compression and union by rank. Space is O(n). This feeds into graph algorithms by providing efficient connectivity queries. Common mistakes include not updating all necessary pointers during union, or creating cycles in the parent pointers. The key insight is that we can represent sets as trees and use parent pointers for efficient membership testing.

examples.trace:
Initial: parent=[0,0,2,3]
find(0): parent[0]=0 → return 0
union(2,3): parent[3]=2 → sets {0,1}, {2,3}
find(3): parent[3]=2, parent[2]=2 → return 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understanding vertices, edges, and how graphs are represented in code.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices. For undirected graphs, edges have no direction - (u,v) is the same as (v,u). Basic terminology: vertex/node (fundamental unit), edge/link (connection between vertices), degree (number of edges incident to a vertex), path (sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge), cycle (path that starts and ends at same vertex with no repeated edges). Graphs can be represented in code using: 1) Adjacency list (list of lists, most common for algorithms), 2) Adjacency matrix (2D array, good for dense graphs), 3) Edge list (list of tuples, simple but less efficient for queries). For Union-Find cycle detection, we typically use edge list representation. Time to create representation is O(V+E). Space is O(V+E) for adjacency list, O(V²) for matrix. This foundation feeds into all graph algorithms. Common mistakes include confusing directed/undirected, off-by-one errors in vertex numbering, or not handling duplicate edges. The intuition is that graphs model relationships, and different representations trade off between memory and query efficiency.

examples.trace:
Vertex 0: connected to 1 → [1]
Vertex 1: connected to 0,2 → [0,2]
Vertex 2: connected to 1,3 → [1,3]
Vertex 3: connected to 2 → [2]
Edge list: [(0,1), (1,2), (2,3)]

Vertex 0: connected to 1,2 → [1,2]
Vertex 1: connected to 0,2 → [0,2]
Vertex 2: connected to 0,1 → [0,1]
Edge list: [(0,1), (1,2), (2,0)]
